metadata.title: Why Every Function You Add to a Nanoparticle Is a New Way to Fail
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Cancer Nanomedicine candidate 04. Source chapter: cancer-nanomedicine/chapters/08-multifunctional-theranostic-nanoparticles.md (the multiplicative-reproducibility / one-vs-six mechanism only).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = simple single-function design / successful translation; CRIMSON = each additional function / batch yield collapse; GOLD = the multiplication number (0.9^6 = 53%)
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: protein corona interaction detail, PDT/PTT/AuroLase gradient, cell-derived particles, full NCI characterization list. Keep 0.9^6 calculation and the one-vs-six contrast.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B13: palette=claude but the outro is 'OutroCTA' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. A research team builds the most sophisticated nanoparticle ever. It images, it heats, it targets, it delivers drug. The paper is celebrated. More than half the manufactured batches fail.

card.copy: Why Every Function You Add to a Nanoparticle Is a New Way to Fail
card.sub: the 0.9 to the sixth power problem

beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] every function is a new way to fail

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [TITLE] every function is a new way to fail

beats.narration_text: Here is the particle: six functions, each working beautifully in the lab. Gold core for heating, iron shell for MRI, a fluorescent dye, a drug payload, a pH-responsive release linker, and a targeting antibody. In mice, it works.

props.headline: Here is the particle: six functions, each working beautifully in the…

beats.scene_description: A schematic diagram of a complex nanoparticle showing six labeled components radiating from a central core, each labeled with its function, rendered like a scientific paper figure pinned to cream newsprint
beats.image_prompt: B02, schematic illustration of a six-function theranostic nanoparticle, central gold core surrounded by six labeled functional layers: iron oxide shell, fluorescent dye, drug payload, pH-responsive linker, targeting antibody, six concentric layers each distinctly colored, editorial flat illustration style, scientific paper figure aesthetic, pinned to aged newsprint background, desaturated scientific diagram

beats.narration_text: Each of those six functions is ninety percent reproducible, batch to batch. Ninety percent. That sounds like high quality. So why does more than half of every manufactured batch fail specification?

card.copy: Six functions. Each 90% reproducible. Why does more than half of every batch fail?

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] gap formula question — 90% per function, majority fail

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] gap formula question — 90% per function, majority fail

beats.narration_text: Because reproducibility does not add — it multiplies. When you need all six functions to pass specification in the same batch, the probabilities compound. Each function is a gate. Every gate must open.

production_viz.label: six gates, all must open
production_viz.mechanic: six vertical gate-bars labeled F1 through F6, each showing 90%; a single particle-dot must pass all six to reach the PASS bucket; gates appear one by one, each with its 90% label
production_viz.note: the gates accumulate — the key visual setup for B05

beats.narration_text: One function at ninety percent: ninety percent pass. Add a second: eighty-one percent. A third: seventy-three percent. Four functions: sixty-six. Five: fifty-nine. Six functions, all at ninety percent each — fifty-three percent pass. More than half your batches fail.

production_viz.label: batch yield as functions stack: 90 81 73 66 59 53
production_viz.mechanic: six bars grow from bottom, each shorter than the last — 90% 81% 73% 66% 59% 53% — labeled F1 through F6 below, percentage above in MONO; bars are CRIMSON; a TEAL reference line at 90% stays fixed
production_viz.note: the collapse graphic — core visual of the film

beats.narration_text: That calculation — point nine to the sixth power equals fifty-three percent — is not a pessimistic assumption. It is what happens at ninety percent per-function quality. Raise it to ninety-five percent per function and you still lose one batch in four.

production_viz.label: 0.9^6 = 53% in large mono type
production_viz.mechanic: the calculation appears in large MONO type: 0.9^6 = 53%; then a second line at 95% shows 0.95^6 = 74%; GOLD highlight bar sweeps the 53% result
production_viz.note: GOLD is fill-only highlighter behind the key number

beats.narration_text: Single-function particles do not face this. Doxil carries one drug payload. Its one function to characterize: encapsulation efficiency and release rate. A radioligand has one function: labeling yield. These are the designs that reached patients.

production_viz.label: one function vs six — the translation contrast
production_viz.mechanic: left side: a simple single bar labeled 'Doxil — 1 function' in TEAL at 90%; right side: the six-bar collapse in CRIMSON ending at 53%; a dividing line between them; TEAL label 'reached patients' on the left, CRIMSON label 'did not translate' on the right
production_viz.note: the one-vs-six contrast — the film's key claim

beats.narration_text: The instinct is to fix this with better manufacturing. Hire process engineers, tighten tolerances. This is the dead end. Process engineering cannot make a six-parameter assembly as consistent as a one-parameter assembly. The math does not care about your engineers.

card.copy: Complexity is a design problem, not a manufacturing problem.
card.sub: no amount of process engineering fixes multiplicative math

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] complexity is a design decision

beats.narration_text: Two manufacturing programs. Program A: a single-function liposome, Doxil-style, ninety percent yield per batch. Twelve batches last year, eleven pass. Program B: six-function theranostic particle. Same per-function quality — but point nine to the sixth means only seven of those twelve batches pass specification.

production_viz.label: Program A vs Program B batch outcomes
production_viz.mechanic: two columns: Program A shows 12 batch squares, 11 TEAL (pass) and 1 CRIMSON (fail); Program B shows 12 batch squares, 7 TEAL and 5 CRIMSON; column labels 'Program A: 1 function' and 'Program B: 6 functions'; count chips below each column
production_viz.note: the illustrative example — numbers are labeled illustrative

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] Program A vs B batch outcomes grid

beats.narration_text: Same per-function quality. Completely different program economics. The six-function particle is not failing because the team is bad at manufacturing. It is failing because the design choices multiplied the failure modes.

production_viz.label: design choice annotation on the batch grid
production_viz.mechanic: the Program A vs B graphic from B09 persists; a HandRing in CRIMSON circles the five failed CRIMSON squares in Program B; a serif annotation label 'design choice' points at the six-function label
production_viz.note: one ring only — points at the consequence of the design choice

beats.new_visual_element: [ANNOTATE] ring on failed batches — design choice

beats.narration_text: The particles that reached patients are the simple ones. Doxil. Abraxane. Radioligands. One function, well characterized, manufactured reproducibly. The elegant six-function Christmas tree particle stays in the paper. Simplification is not a concession. It is the work.

card.copy: More functions means more ways to fail. Simple designs translate. Complex ones do not.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] endcard — simple designs translate

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Cancer Nanomedicine series.
